summary: Joel Spolsky argues that three widely-accepted ideas from computer science are fundamentally wrong: that search should maximize results, that anti-aliased text looks better, and that network resources should behave like local ones. He points out that Google succeeded by focusing on ranking rather than recall, that anti-aliased body text looks blurry compared to pixel-fitted fonts like Verdana, and that network transparency abstractions like RPC and DCOM dangerously hide latency, availability, and reliability concerns. The post challenges the gap between academic ideals and practical engineering reality.
key_points: Search engines like Altavista failed by optimizing for recall rather than relevance; Google's PageRank succeeded by focusing on sorting results, not finding more of them.
Anti-aliased text looks worse for sustained reading at screen resolution; fonts like Verdana that embrace the pixel grid are more readable than anti-aliased high-resolution fonts.
Network transparency abstractions like RPC and DCOM hide critical differences between local and remote resources: availability, latency, and reliability.
Practical engineering must account for real-world constraints rather than pursuing theoretically elegant abstractions that ignore them.
Microsoft's ClearType, despite being a newer approach to anti-aliasing, still produces blurry text on LCD screens.
money_quotes: By reducing the search from one billion to one million pages, Altavista has done absolutely nothing for me.
The real problem in searching is how to sort the results.
Ranking the pages in the right order was more important than grabbing every possible page.
If you try to read a paragraph of antialiased text, it just looks blurry. There's nothing I can do about it, it's the truth.
Instead of creating a high-resolution font and then trying to hammer it into the pixel grid, they finally accepted the pixel grid as a 'given' and designed a font that fits neatly into it.
Using programming interfaces that hide all this stuff from you is a great way to make a lousy software program.
The next time someone tries to sell you a programming product that lets you access network resources the same way as you access local resources, run full speed in the opposite direction.
key_insight: Elegant computer science abstractions that hide real-world complexity — whether in search, rendering, or networking — produce worse outcomes than pragmatic solutions that embrace the underlying constraints.
